speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my parents when they got married. My father's name is Saul Brawer-- Yisrael. And my mother's name is Mina Huss. And it took place in 1910. My-- this is my uncle, my father's baby brother. His name was Maurice. And when the picture was taken, I don't know. But it was taken in Lwow. And the other one is also my father's brother. His name was Michael-- Michael. And I also don't know when that was taken. At the right side is my mother. And next is my aunt, my mother's sister. And she lived in Argentina, and my mother went to visit her. So this was taken-- I don't know what year, but this was taken in Buenos Aires.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of my aunt was Faige. My mother's name is Mina. This is going to be in the side-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know that picture is a triumph. Because it's you, and you came through everything. And so it's a triumph.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm-- one second. [INAUDIBLE]. And this is myself, after the war I, maybe a couple months after. I made it for identification. This was my first photo after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is the person in the picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sauer, I see you're sitting there with all these very beautiful people. Could you tell us who they are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes I can. This is my first born, this is Marlene. And she was born in Vienna in 1948. Next is Dana, is my granddaughter, it's Marlene's daughter. And she was born in Los Angeles in 1973. DANA (GRANDDAUGHTER): Well, 4.

speaker: interviewee
text: '74. [LAUGHTER] This is my husband, Joe, Joseph. He was born in Poland. And he was born in 1920. 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long have you been married now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. We got married in 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow. So next, of course-- I mean, this is my son, Steve. And he was born in Los Angeles in 1952. This is Jonathan, Steve's son. Jonathan was born in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles. Next is [? Mikki, ?] my daughter-in-law. And she was born in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: In '54. And then is Molly, my granddaughter, my son's daughter. She was born in--

speaker: interviewee
text: My son's 1939 birthday. They have the same birthday. And of course, in Los Angeles. Did I say? And then is Angie, my eldest granddaughter. And she was born in Los Angeles, in 1971. November 1st.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now would anyone in the family like to say a few words perhaps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've never been short on words. So I'll crowd up next to my mother's microphone. I'm very pleased that my grandmother decided-- my mother decided to give this interview. And we're still encouraging my father to do so. I think it's very important that not only my generation but my children's generation learns more about what happened, and the atrocities that occurred to Jews and non-Jews alike, during a very horrible period of time in the world. And I hope that these types of testimonials and interviews are studied and looked at and can be concerned about by the world, so that this sort of thing never happens again.

speaker: interviewee
text: (WHISPERING) Do you want to say something about Nanny's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Molly, do you want to say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: Molly, would you like? And Jonathan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jonathan would like to say something.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, and thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. I goofed up--
